CIS3521 B Final Project: Mangrove Tree Data Analysis

Flagler College 2026

Author

Ava Mariano, Jazmine Major, Gavin Hines

1. Introduction

Our data set came from a Flagler senior Capstone project involving the sampling of Mangrove trees from seven distinct sites, which were anayzed at the University of Hawai’i at Hilo’s Analytical Labratory. Data included in the labratory test results which we chose to analyze were the sample ID’s and their % of Organic Matter (OM), Nitrogen (N), and Carbon (C).

2. Data Cleaning

We extracted a number of raw data columns from capstone excel sheet and entered the information into a new excel sheet for easier cleaning. Column names were changed to lower snake case, NA columns were removed, names were corrected and made uniform (e.g. percent_c -> c_percent), and species and site columns were created using mutate(). Typos and spacing errors were fixed, and superfluous comments from original athors were removed (e.g. “Very Shelly” written in weighboat_code). Typos and comments were fixed using str_replace_all and str_remove, furthermore, in creating new columns, str_start and str_detect were found in Tidyverse’s stringr package documentation.

library(tidyverse)

library(janitor)

library(readxl)

library(tinytable)

library(ggpubr)


plant_material_samples <- read_csv("/Users/gavinhines/Documents/CIS351 B 2026 Spring/Data/Final_Project_Data(Sheet1).csv") 

plant_material_samples <- clean_names(plant_material_samples)

plant_material_samples <- plant_material_samples |> 
    rename(
        weighboat_code = weigh_boat_code,
        c_percent = percent_c,
        n_percent = percent_n
    ) 

plant_material_samples_2 <- plant_material_samples |> 
    filter_out(is.na(weighboat_code)) 

plant_material_samples_3 <- plant_material_samples_2 |> 
    mutate(
        weighboat_code = str_replace_all(weighboat_code, "FBI", "FRI"),

        weighboat_code = str_remove(weighboat_code, "-.*$"),
        # looked this up: what its doing is removing all characters after the hyphen no matter what it is continuously until the end of the string ($). This was to remove the extra comments left in .xslx file
        weighboat_code = recode(weighboat_code, "BTOY2A" = "B TOY 2A")
    )

C and N % were calculated using OM% in a formula. These formulas required OM as a decimal, but it seems capstone students used a %. It is unknown what errors were made with the output of these formulas, but it probably involved improper unit conversion. By researching normal C and N % of mangrove trees, we were able to modify the results of the formulas to bring them into realistic ranges. The exact formulas they used were extracted from the capstone excel sheet.

plant_material_samples_4 <- plant_material_samples_3 |> 
    mutate( 
        #creating 'species' categorical variable from 'weighboat_code'
        species = case_when(
            str_starts(weighboat_code, "R") ~ "Red Mangrove", 
            str_starts(weighboat_code, "B") ~ "Black Mangrove"
        ),
        # creating 'site' categorical variable from 'weighboat_code'
        site = case_when(
            str_detect(weighboat_code, "SUN") ~ "SUN",
            str_detect(weighboat_code, "MON") ~ "MON",
            str_detect(weighboat_code, "TAN") ~ "TAN",
            str_detect(weighboat_code, "LOB") ~ "LOB",
            str_detect(weighboat_code, "TOY") ~ "TOY",
            str_detect(weighboat_code, "STR") ~ "STR",
            str_detect(weighboat_code, "FRI") ~ "FRI"
        ),
        # capstone students used formulas and did not use the proper units, that's why N% is sometimes listed > 700%
        c_percent = ((0.2385 * om_percent) + 0.7742) * 10, # to make % match normal mangrove levels
        n_percent = ((16.962 * om_percent) - 0.3475) / 100 # from g/kg to %
    )

3. Descripitive Statistics

We provide summary statistics for the numerical variables: om_percent, c_percent, and n_percent. For the categorical variables, we measured summary statistics for species and site.

3.1 Summary Stats for species

plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    filter(!is.na(species)) |> 
    summarize(
        Count = n(),
        .by = species
    ) |> 
        tt()
species Count
Red Mangrove 60
Black Mangrove 66

3.2 Summary Stats for site

plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    filter(!is.na(site)) |> 
    summarize(
        Count = n(),
        .by = site
    ) |> 
        tt()
site Count
SUN 15
MON 21
TAN 18
LOB 18
TOY 18
STR 18
FRI 18

3.3 Summary Stats for om_percent

plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    filter(!is.na(om_percent) & om_percent > 0) |> 
    summarize(
        min_om = min(om_percent),
        median_om = median(om_percent),
        mean_om = round(mean(om_percent), 1),
        max_om = max(om_percent),
        sd_om = round(sd(om_percent), 1)
    ) |> 
    tt()
min_om median_om mean_om max_om sd_om
1.796889 9.943196 16.2 73.14936 17

3.4 Summary Stats for c_percent

plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    filter(!is.na(c_percent) & c_percent > 0) |> 
    summarize(
        min_c = min(c_percent),
        median_c = median(c_percent),
        mean_c = round(mean(c_percent), 1),
        max_c = max(c_percent),
        sd_c = round(sd(c_percent), 1)
    ) |> 
    tt()
min_c median_c mean_c max_c sd_c
12.02758 31.45652 46.3 182.2032 40.4

3.5 Summary Stats for n_percent

plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    filter(!is.na(n_percent) & n_percent > 0) |> 
    summarize(
        min_n = min(n_percent),
        median_n = median(n_percent),
        mean_n = round(mean(n_percent), 1),
        max_n = max(n_percent),
        sd_n = round(sd(n_percent), 1)
    ) |> 
    tt()
min_n median_n mean_n max_n sd_n
0.3013133 1.68309 2.7 12.40412 2.9

4. Data Visualizations

4.1 Stacked Bar Chart of species and site

This stacked bar chart shows the count of Red or Black Mangrove species by sampling site. We can see it is generally consistent across all seven sites.

plot_om_bar_2 <- plant_material_samples_4 |> 
  filter(!is.na(species)) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = site, fill = species)) +
  geom_bar() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Red Mangrove" = "firebrick2", "Black Mangrove" = "grey25")) + # to specifically choose colors of species
  labs(
    title = "Mangrove Species by Site",
    x = "Sample Site",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
    theme_pubclean() +
    theme(legend.position = "none") # color legend woud be redundant


plot_om_bar_2

4.2 Histogram

We provide a histogram showcasing the distribution of Organic Matter % across all samples. The histrogram shows us om_percent across samples is heavily right skewed, meaning that most samples taken have low OM values, and a few have much higher values.

plot_om <- plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    filter(om_percent > 0) |> 
    ggplot(aes(x = om_percent))+ 
        geom_histogram(
            binwidth = 3,
            fill = 'aquamarine',
            color = 'blue'
     )+
    labs(
        x = "Organic Matter (%)",
        y = "Count",
        title = "OM% Count of Plant Samples"
    )+
           theme_pubclean()+
    # The title of histogram needs to be more visible 
    theme(
        plot.title = element_text(size = 16), 
        plot.margin = margin (t = 20)
    )
    
plot_om

4.3 Box Plot of om_percent by species

This graph shows use how Organic Matter (%) differs by species. As we can see, Black mangrove trees have higher OM% on average than Red Mangrove, but its variance is way higher.

plant_om_sum <- plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    filter(!is.na(om_percent) & om_percent > 0) |>
    summarize(
        mean_om_p = mean(om_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd_om_p = sd(om_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = species
    )

plot_om_bar <- plant_om_sum |> 
  ggplot() +
  geom_bar(
    aes(x = species, y = mean_om_p, fill = species),
    stat = 'identity' 
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x = species, ymin = mean_om_p - sd_om_p, ymax = mean_om_p + sd_om_p),
    color = 'orange',
    alpha = 0.5,
    width = 0.1,
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Red Mangrove" = "firebrick2", "Black Mangrove" = "grey25")) + # to specifically choose colors of species
  labs(
    title = "Mean Organic Matter by Species",
    subtitle = "Error bar represent standard error",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Organic Matter (%)"
  ) +
    theme_pubclean() +
    theme(legend.position = "none") # color legend woud be redundant

plot_om_bar

4.4 Scatter Plot of average om_percent vs average n_percent by site

In the original data, Carbon and Nitrogen % were calculated using a formula with the plant sample’s Organic Matter % as an input. This guarantees a linear relationship between OM, C, and N (hence, R = 1). So, the purpose of this scatter plot is to look at how the % of Nitrogen and Organic Matter differ by site. Since high nitrogen density is a sign of a healthy mangrove tree, this scatter plot compares the mean nitrogen of each site with this in mind. The error bars show the variance of each site’s mean. We can see that MON and LOB likely have the healthiest environments for a mangrove tree, and the other sites probably have more difficulties such as lots of sand in the ground.

plant_om_n_sum <- plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    summarize(
        mean_om_p = mean(om_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd_om_p = sd(om_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
        mean_n_p = mean(n_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd_n_p = sd(n_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = site
    )

plot_om_n <- plant_om_n_sum |> 
    ggplot(
        aes(
            x = mean_om_p, y = mean_n_p
           )  
    )+
    # shows how variable nitrogen content is at site
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_n_p - sd_n_p, ymax = mean_n_p + sd_n_p), color = "grey", width = 0)+
    geom_point(
        aes(
            color = site
           ),
        alpha = 1,
        size = 5
    )+

    geom_smooth(
        method = 'lm', #linear model
        color = 'red',
        linewidth = 0.5 ,
        se = FALSE
    )+
    # learned how to use this function because ylim() kept causing problems by itself
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 10))+

    stat_regline_equation(
        label.x = 25,
        label.y = 9,
        size = 6
    )+

    stat_cor(
        label.x = 25,
        label.y = 8,
        size = 6
    )+

    labs(
        title = "Mean Nitrogen Density by Site",
        subtitle = "Error Bars show standard error. MON & LOB show highest nutrient density",
        x = 'Mean Organic Matter (%)',
        y = 'Mean Nitrogen (%)'
    )+

    theme_pubclean()

plot_om_n

4.5 Percent Stacked Bar Chart of site and om_percent

The higher the amount of organic matter a plan has, the higher its Carbon and Nitrogen content will be. As such, by measuring the organic matter density of each site, we can determine each sites nutrient potential relative to the other sites. We can see that the sites LOB and MON have the highest nutrient potential, as their average OM% is greater than or equal to 66% of all om_percent measurements taken.

plant_om_sum2 <- plant_material_samples_4 |> 
    summarize(
        mean_om_p = mean(om_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd_om_p = sd(om_percent, na.rm = TRUE),
        .by = site
    )

plant_om_bar_3 <- plant_material_samples_4 |> 
  filter(!is.na(om_percent) & !is.na(site)) |> 
  mutate(
    om_density = case_when(
      om_percent >= quantile(om_percent, 0.66) ~ 'High',
      om_percent >= quantile(om_percent, 0.33) ~ 'Medium',
      TRUE ~ 'Low'
    ),
    om_density = factor(om_density, levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High" ))
  ) |> 
  ggplot(
    aes(
      x = site,
      fill = om_density
    )
  ) +
  geom_bar(
    position = 'fill',
    color = 'black'
  ) +
  theme_pubclean()+
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = 'YlGn') +
  labs(
    title = 'Percent Stacked Bar Chart of Site and Nutrient Potential',
    x = 'Site',
    y = 'Nutrient Potential',
    fill = "Organic Matter Density"
  )

plant_om_bar_3

5. Statisical Tests

5.1 Kruskal-Wallace Test

It’s evident in our graphs that om_percent differs across sites. We will run a Kruskal-Wallace test to confirm how significant this difference across sites is:

k_test <- kruskal.test(om_percent ~ site, data = plant_material_samples_4)

print(k_test)

    Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test

data:  om_percent by site
Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared = 70.634, df = 6, p-value = 3.031e-13

Our hypotheses are:

  • \(H_0\): The distribution of organic matter percentage is the same across all sites.
  • \(H_a\): The distribution of organic matter percentage is not equal across all sites.

The \(p\)-value is \(3.031 \times 10^{-13} < 0.05 = \alpha\). Therefore, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is significant evidence that organic matter percentage differs across the sampling sites.

5.2 Rank-Sum Test

Now, to use another non-parametic test that works across two categorical variables, we will use the Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test. It seemed in our graphs that there was not a strong relationship between species and OM%, so we will test to see how accurate that observation is:

rs_test <- wilcox.test(om_percent ~ species, data = plant_material_samples_4)

print(rs_test)

    Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction

data:  om_percent by species
W = 1598, p-value = 0.2301
alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0

Our hypotheses are:

  • \(H_0\): The distribution of organic matter percentage is the same across both species.
  • \(H_a\): The distribution of organic matter percentage is not equal across both species.

The \(p\)-value is \(0.2301 > 0.05 = \alpha\). Therefore, we fail to reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is not significant evidence that organic matter percentage differs between the two species.

6. Conclusion

We found that there is significant evidence of Organic Matter % differing across sites, and that there is not significant evidence of Organic Matter % differing across species. In other words, Organic Matter content of Mangrove tree samples differs across environments. With higher Organic Matter comes higher nutrient potential for plants in the form of Carbon and Nitrogen.

The limitations of this study included the lack of lab-tested C% and N% results. In this Capstone project, these values were derived from OM% using a formula. Since formulas were used, a linear relationship between these values is inevitable. With lab-tested results, we could have analyzed the relationship between C%, N%, and OM% in a more meaningful way.

Another limitation of our study was the lack of normal distribution in our data. Since our plant sampling data was not normally distributed, we could not effectively test our hypotheses using stronger statistical tests that require normal distribution such as the parametric tests, t-test and ANOVA.

It would be worth studying the environment of sampling sites with high OM% v.s. those of low OM% for potential future research, as when the difference in their environments can be found, it will provide clues as to the healthiest circumstances for Mangrove trees.
